reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher professional autonomy, workload feasibility, and implementation clarity are core interests of the teaching profession. S1 imposes new assessment design and administration burdens without demonstrating they are sustainable or that autonomy over classroom practice is preserved.
reactions.interest: Hungarian teachers' workload, professional autonomy in assessment, and implementation feasibility
reactions.public_good_frame: Educational equity is a legitimate public goal, but it must be achieved without degrading teaching conditions or imposing unvalidated assessment regimes that shift burden onto schools.
reactions.argument: S1 requires Oktatási Hivatal to design a 'coaching-resistant assessment' by year 3, but the scenario itself labels this assumption 'not established by cited value-added evidence.' Teachers will inherit an assessment whose psychometric properties are untested at scale. Simultaneously, outreach and quota administration add compliance burden without corresponding relief elsewhere. The conditional support depends on three non-negotiable conditions: (1) the new assessment must be demonstrably less preparation-sensitive than the current exam — not theoretically, but in a multi-year pilot showing coaching has measurably less effect; (2) administration of reserved-seat quotas and appeals must not fall on schools as an unfunded mandate; and (3) standards must remain guidance, not compliance benchmarks, preserving teacher judgment in heterogeneous classrooms. The scenario flags 'standards are published as guidance, not compliance benchmarks, so teacher unions do not defect' — this is not an assumption, it is a hard precondition.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support becomes opposition if: (a) the new assessment is rolled out before independent evaluators publish pilot data showing coaching resistance; (b) quota verification and appeals administration becomes a school-level unfunded mandate; (c) standards are later shifted to compliance benchmarks, triggering performance audits linked to teacher evaluation; or (d) outreach campaigns are funded nationally but schools are expected to absorb administrative overhead. Support becomes stronger if: (e) a parallel study track shows that the new assessment does not require test-prep coaching and reduces tutoring demand; (f) quota administration remains entirely at the ministry level with transparent, published audit trails; (g) there is explicit contractual guarantee that standards remain guidance; and (h) early pilot data from year 2-3 show that low-SES application rates rise without requiring increased teacher involvement in outreach.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher workload, job security, and reallocation burden. S2 imposes a decade-long dual-regime administration, requires staff redistribution from selective to general schools, and creates sustained uncertainty about school viability and employment stability.
reactions.interest: Teachers in both selective and general schools — job security, workload predictability, and professional standing
reactions.public_good_frame: Education reform must maintain working conditions and professional stability; a decade of transition uncertainty undermines teacher retention and morale, harming service quality for all pupils, including disadvantaged ones.
reactions.argument: S2 creates a 10-year parallel regime in which selective-track teachers face declining enrollment (grandfathered cohorts run out) while general-school teachers absorb incoming pupils in expanded upper grades. The scenario acknowledges 'absorption capacity in general-school upper grades (staffing, space)' is a medium-to-high cost, but it offers no redeployment guarantee or career-protection framework. Teachers in selective schools face a professional status loss and redeployment insecurity that is likely to accelerate exits precisely when teaching supply is already critically short. The scenario's own uncertainty — 'Will non-state maintainers absorb the capped selective demand?' — means that caps may fail, or succeed by pushing demand to church/foundation schools, leaving state selective-school staff in limbo. A decade-long process of managed decline is an implementation model that leaves teachers in both tracks experiencing lost autonomy and job insecurity. Additionally, the scenario's own political-risk section flags that 'residual selective places become even more SES-skewed as they scarcen,' meaning selective-track teachers will be asked to manage even higher-pressure, higher-conflict admissions — contradicting any claim that the reform improves their working conditions.
reactions.condition_to_change: Opposition becomes conditional only if: (a) a binding, multi-year employment guarantee is legislated for all teachers in closing selective tracks, protecting salary and offering lateral redeployment to general schools with no pay loss; (b) redeployment is voluntary, not forced, and teachers who choose early exit receive enhanced severance; (c) general-school upper grades receive additional staffing allocations (not redeployment, but new posts) to prevent workload inflation; (d) the timeline is shortened to 5 years rather than 10, reducing prolonged uncertainty; and (e) parallel union-ministry labor negotiations are visible and published before parliamentary approval. Opposition becomes stronger support only if a pilot demonstration in one region shows that dual-regime management is less burdensome than predicted and that non-state offset does not materialize.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher supply, professional retraining burden, and workload in heterogeneous classes. S3 requires nationwide retraining in differentiated instruction and abolishes structural streaming, forcing all teachers to manage much wider achievement ranges without demonstrated supply-side capacity.
reactions.interest: The teaching profession — workload sustainability, professional development on a realistic timeline, and realistic assessment of teacher supply constraints
reactions.public_good_frame: Structural reform toward comprehensive schooling is educationally coherent, but it is not feasible in Hungary's current teacher-shortage context; attempting it without solving supply will harm both disadvantaged and advantaged pupils by overloading teachers and triggering exit.
reactions.argument: S3 is the most demand-intensive reform for the teaching profession. It requires 'nationwide teacher retraining in differentiated instruction' (cost: high, per the scenario) by year 6, while teacher supply is already critically constrained. The scenario's own implementation-planning inputs flag 'strong teacher-shortage finding,' and the uncertainties section explicitly states 'Can teacher supply support heterogeneous classes at national scale? [confidence: low].' The scenario acknowledges that sorting may 'simply migrate to school choice between general schools and to non-state maintainers,' meaning retraining investments may not even achieve the intended equity outcome — teachers may be forced to retrain for comprehensive classes while pupils exit to private or church schools anyway. Additionally, S3 bundles the comprehensive structure with 'standardized teacher training and early diagnostic support investment,' which the scenario marks as a precondition ('Bundling with standardized teacher training... prevents within-class variance from driving middle-class flight'). This means there is no decoupling: if political will for bundled investment collapses, the structural reform proceeds anyway, leaving teachers to manage heterogeneous classes without the promised diagnostic support. The scenario itself flags that 'teacher-union defection if standards are bundled with performance audits' is a moderate political risk — this is not a vague risk, it is a red flag that the bundle is unacceptable to the profession. Opposition is near-certain.
reactions.condition_to_change: Opposition becomes conditional only if: (a) a 10-year teacher-supply forecast is published showing that new entry and retention will actually stabilize to sustainable levels before national rollout, not merely by year 6; (b) 'bundled investment' is legally binding across electoral cycles, with dedicated revenue (tax or bond-backed) that cannot be cut in fiscal consolidation; (c) retraining is entirely voluntary with no performance-audit component, and teachers who do not retrain are not penalized or reassigned; (d) a full-scale pilot in at least two regions runs for 4 years before any national rollout, with published union input on workload outcomes; and (e) non-state maintainers are brought into the common-phase mandate (not merely negotiated with), so that middle-class flight does not turn comprehensive schools into residualized institutions. Opposition becomes stronger if early pilot data show high teacher exit from comprehensive schools or if any government attempts to decouple the structural reform from the bundled investment.

reactions.label: no_position
reactions.interest: Teachers' workload and professional standing — these interests do not imply a clear stance on S4 because S4 does not directly alter teacher roles, qualifications, or job security in the structural way that S1-S3 do.
reactions.public_good_frame: S4 is a within-sector compensation approach that does not touch the streaming mechanism or require teacher retraining; it is orthogonal to most teacher-profession interests.
reactions.argument: S4 retains the 6/8-year structure and routes targeted funding and curriculum standards to disadvantaged general schools. For the teaching profession, this scenario has mixed and unclear implications. On one hand, it avoids the redeployment, retraining, and job-insecurity burdens that S2 and S3 impose. On the other hand, it does not relieve the core structural problem that teachers in disadvantaged general schools face: they inherit a stream of lower-motivated and lower-prepared pupils because the selective-track selection has drained high-achievers, and compensation funding does not change that pupil composition. The scenario itself labels the equity gain as 'at best partial' because it 'leaves the sorting mechanism itself intact.' Teachers have no stake in whether the mechanism is intact or reformed — they have a stake in workload and pupil composition. S4 does not improve pupil composition in disadvantaged general schools (it only adds resources), so it does not relieve the underlying pressure. Conversely, S4 does not impose new unfunded mandates or retraining on teachers, so it does not create the opposition drivers that S1-S3 trigger. The teaching profession as an interest group has no documented position on whether compensation-only approaches are preferable to structural reform — this is a policy question that does not map onto professional interests in workload, autonomy, or job security. The scenario's own risks (targeted funding being 'the first line cut in fiscal consolidation,' and 'teacher shortage in disadvantaged schools undermines delivery') are resource problems, not profession-specific stances. Silence here is honest.
